title: Pfizer — Overview
html:
Pfizer Account Overview

Governing Thought

Pfizer is ZoomRx's highest-growth account — a $302K FY25 engagement targeting 90% growth to $575K in FY26 — anchored in a vaccines franchise that spans RSV (Abrysvo), pneumococcal (Prevnar 20), and meningococcal (Penbraya) with emerging footholds in oncology (Lorbrena PET) and above-brand AI (Message Labs pilot). The account's complexity is different from J&J's: instead of breadth across many TAs, the challenge is depth within vaccines and the strategic imperative to break out of vaccines into Pfizer's primary care, specialty, and oncology divisions. Marketing leads hold vendor decisions at the brand level, and brands operate independently — making franchise-level selling impossible and requiring a brand-by-brand entry strategy. The HCPP (HCP-Patient Conversations) methodology is the wedge that opens doors; AI reputation is the accelerant that creates above-brand visibility; and the portfolio-level insights from cross-study analysis (communication style, brand-agnostic recommendations, disease severity gaps) are the strategic currency that elevates ZoomRx from research vendor to insights partner.

The Pfizer Landscape: What You Need to Know

Pfizer at a Glance

Dimension Detail
AO Rich Durante (Principal)
AD Nick Fleischman (SM)
FY25 Base $302,250 (61% profitability)
FY26 Target $575,000 (90% growth)
Therapeutic Areas Vaccines (primary), Primary Care, Specialty, Oncology
Brands with active ZoomRx work Abrysvo (Maternal + OA), Prevnar 20, Trumenba/Penbraya, Lorbrena, plus AI Migraine Pilot
Active project types HCPP, PET, ATU, Digital Tracking, Message Labs, Pharmacist Quant, Pulse Survey
Vinoth's role Overseen account

Key dynamics:

  • Marketing leads hold significant sway and are very involved in vendor decisions
  • Brands are structured independently, making franchise plays difficult
  • HCP-Pt Conversations becoming entrenched — 4 different vaccine products using it
  • AI capabilities on Abrysvo PETs making ZoomRx known as AI pioneers/experts
  • Message Labs project (AI Migraine Messaging Pilot) represents first non-vaccine entry

Account Strategy

Core approach: Break into new teams using HCPP as the hook, then expand to PET/ATU using Abrysvo and Seagen/Pfizer oncology as use cases.

FY26 Priorities:

  1. Use HCPP vaccine engagements as use cases to expand and create advocates (Q1)
  2. Push to expand into PET space with zero-cost pilots (Q2)
  3. Enter 2+ non-vaccine brands to fill the $125K "other brands" target
  4. Deepen multi-service relationships on existing vaccine brands (HCPP + PET + ATU + Digital)

Vaccines Portfolio (Primary Revenue Driver)

Vaccines generate 100% of FY25 revenue and ~78% of FY26 target revenue. This is the franchise where ZoomRx has deep credibility and institutional knowledge.

Abrysvo (RSV Vaccine) — Maternal + Older Adults

  • What it is: Bivalent prefusion F protein subunit vaccine (RSV A + RSV B); the only RSV vaccine approved for both older adults and maternal immunization
  • Indications: Prevention of RSV-associated LRTD in adults 60+ (universal 75+, shared decision 50-74 at risk); prevention in infants via maternal immunization (32-36 weeks gestation, Sep-Jan)
  • Label expansion: FDA approved for adults 18-59 at increased risk (2024)
  • FY 2025 Revenue: ~$1B globally; Q4 2025 showed 136% operational growth driven by international launch uptake
  • Key clinical data: RENOIR trial: 66.7% efficacy against RSV-LRTD in adults 60+; 78.8% against severe RSV LRTD. Maternal trial: 81.8% efficacy against severe RSV LRTD in infants within first 90 days
  • Key competitors: Arexvy (GSK, adjuvanted, first-to-market, ~$785M in 2025); mResvia (Moderna, mRNA, struggling with $8M 2025 sales)
  • Brand AOR: [Populate]
  • ZoomRx work: PET (Maternal, $150K; OA, $75K), HCPP (Maternal recurring, OA completed), Digital Tracking (Maternal)

Key challenges:

  • CDC narrowed the older adult recommendation in 2024 — from all 60+ (shared decision) to 75+ universal / 50-74 at risk. This reduced the target population and created HCP confusion
  • Maternal market facing competition from nirsevimab (Beyfortus, Sanofi/AZ) monoclonal antibody — US coverage data shows 38%+ via nirsevimab vs. 19% via maternal vaccination
  • Seasonal revenue concentration (Aug-Jan vaccination window) creates Q-to-Q volatility
  • ZoomRx HCPP finding: 8/10 HCPs make brand-agnostic RSV vaccine recommendations; only 2/10 name a specific brand (neither was Abrysvo)

Prevnar 20 (PCV20, Pneumococcal)

  • What it is: 20-valent pneumococcal conjugate vaccine; market leader in adult pneumococcal vaccination
  • Indications: Adults 18+ (single dose); pediatric 6 weeks-17 years (series)
  • FY 2025 Revenue: Prevnar family ~$6B+ annually; 8% operational growth Q4 2025; 3 billionth dose milestone
  • Market expansion: October 2024 ACIP expanded recommendation from 65+ to all adults 50+ (PCV-naive) — added ~30 million vaccine-eligible adults
  • Key competitors: Capvaxive (PCV21, Merck, 21 serotypes, claims 84% IPD coverage in 50+ vs. 52% for Prevnar 20); Vaxneuvance (PCV15, Merck, legacy); VAX-31 (Vaxcyte, 31-valent, Phase 3, potential disruptor post-2027)
  • ZoomRx work: HCPP (Prevnar Adult, targeting 50-64 age group), Pharmacist Quant

Key competitive dynamic — Serotype Counting: ZoomRx data shows the "21 > 20 = better" framing is emerging in real conversations. When both PCV20 and PCV21 are discussed, Capvaxive is accepted in 3/7 cases. One HCP told a patient: "Prevnar 20 gives you 52% protection... Capvaxive gives you 84%." Prevnar 20's serotype 4 advantage (prevalent in the US, NOT covered by Capvaxive) is never once communicated to patients in any recorded conversation. This serotype counting narrative is the most commercially urgent competitive threat in the vaccines portfolio.

Penbraya (MenABCWY, Meningococcal Pentavalent)

  • What it is: Pentavalent meningococcal vaccine combining components of Trumenba (MenB) and Nimenrix (MenACWY)
  • Indications: Ages 10-25; two-dose series (0, 6 months) replacing what was previously 4-5 separate injections
  • Key value proposition: Simplification — reduces total injections, potentially improving MenB completion rates (historically ~30% among 17-year-olds vs. ~90% for MenACWY)
  • Key competitor: GSK's pentavalent (Bexsero + Menveo combined) — also ACIP recommended
  • ZoomRx work: HCPP (Meningococcal study), ATU (first Pfizer ATU), Digital Tracking (in discussion)

Strategic question: Is low MenB uptake caused by dosing complexity (which Penbraya solves) or by MenB apathy (which it doesn't)? ZoomRx's thesis is the latter — meaning Penbraya needs a market development strategy for MenB awareness before the simplification pitch can land.

Non-Vaccine Portfolio (Expansion Targets)

These divisions represent the growth frontier. ZoomRx has zero to minimal current revenue here, but the AO plan targets $125K from "other brands" in FY26.

Primary Care

Brand Category ZoomRx Status Entry Path
Paxlovid COVID antiviral No active project; Gavin Hansen is warm (mutual referral) HCPP pitch — complex patient decision-making (COVID fear vs. drug concerns)
Nurtec Migraine (CGRP) Message Labs AI Pilot via Aimee Renaud; no direct brand contact Via Tim Douglas or Aimee Renaud intro
Eliquis Anticoagulant No contact; mature brand with entrenched vendors Low priority — only if contact surfaces organically

Specialty Care

Brand Category ZoomRx Status Entry Path
Xeljanz JAK inhibitor (RA, PsA, UC) No contact Via Aimee Renaud or Josh Stewart referral
Vyndaquel TTR stabilizer (cardiac amyloidosis) No contact Via Josh Stewart (rare disease)

Oncology (Seagen Integration)

Brand Category ZoomRx Status Entry Path
Lorbrena ALK+ NSCLC PET active (#13) — first oncology project Oncology contact TBD
Ibrance CDK4/6 inhibitor (breast cancer) No contact; ~$4B+ annual brand Long-term; oncology org is separate
Xtandi AR inhibitor (prostate) No contact Oncology contact development required
Padcev Nectin-4 ADC (bladder/urothelial) No contact Most interesting Seagen asset; recent launches
Adcetris CD30 ADC (Hodgkin lymphoma) No contact Oncology contact development required
Tukysa HER2 inhibitor (breast/CRC) No contact Long-term

Pipeline to Watch

These assets will generate new project requests as they advance. Flag them in account planning conversations.

Asset Mechanism Stage Why It Matters
VAX-31 (Vaxcyte) 31-valent PCV Phase 3 (FDA Breakthrough) Potential best-in-class; >95% IPD coverage; could disrupt Prevnar 20 AND Capvaxive post-2027. If Pfizer needs to defend against VAX-31, ZoomRx's competitive tracking capabilities become critical.
Clesrovimab Anti-RSV monoclonal (infants) Late-stage competitor to nirsevimab Further threatens Abrysvo maternal market if another monoclonal enters
Pfizer obesity pipeline GLP-1 candidate (danuglipron) Early development Candace Anderson is warm contact. If Pfizer launches an obesity product, HCPP would be a natural first engagement.
Pfizer oncology (Seagen) Multiple ADCs and novel agents Various The Seagen acquisition massively expanded Pfizer's oncology portfolio. Lorbrena PET is the beachhead.
RSV booster data Abrysvo booster durability Clinical development If booster data emerges, the "one and done" narrative changes and recurring RSV vaccination becomes a market; could also drive new tracking work.

ZoomRx's Service Footprint

The matrix below shows the breadth of ZoomRx engagement across Pfizer's brand portfolio.

Service Abrysvo Maternal Abrysvo OA Prevnar Adult Trumenba/Penbraya Lorbrena Non-Vaccine
PET / SFEA X ($150K) X ($75K) X
HCP-Patient (HCPP) X X X X
ATU X
Digital Tracking X (discussing)
Message Labs X (AI Migraine)
Pharmacist Quant (cross-portfolio)
Pulse Survey (cross-portfolio)

Key takeaway: Abrysvo Maternal is the most deeply serviced brand (PET + HCPP + Digital Tracking). Other brands have 1-2 services each. The upsell opportunity is significant: adding ATU to Abrysvo Maternal and PET to Prevnar Adult / Trumenba would dramatically deepen the relationship. The non-vaccine column has exactly one project — Message Labs — making it the thinnest part of the footprint and the highest-priority growth area.

Competitive Landscape Summary

Understanding vendor dynamics is essential because growth depends on both new wins and competitive displacement.

Vendor Competition

Area Incumbent ZoomRx Position Displacement Strategy
PET BI (most Pfizer PETs) PET active on Abrysvo only; Lorbrena PET as oncology entry Flank via HCPP-to-PET upsell; zero-cost pilot on Trumenba or Prevnar Adult in Q2; AI-enhanced analytics as differentiator
HCP-Patient Verilogue (select brands) HCPP on 4 vaccine brands — dominant in vaccines Differentiate on AI-powered output (themes, recommendations) vs. Verilogue's transcripts-and-coding; coexist on some brands, displace on others
ATU Sermo (Abrysvo Maternal ATU) First ATU launched (Meningococcal) Integrated insights story: one vendor providing HCPP + PET + ATU = three lenses on the same market

Brand-Level Competitive Dynamics

Brand Primary Competitor Competitive Dynamic Implication for ZoomRx Work
Abrysvo (Adult) Arexvy (GSK) GSK is first-to-market with higher 2025 sales but declining; CDC narrowing hurt all 3 players; brand-agnostic HCP recommendations dominate HCPP reveals that brand differentiation is the key challenge; brand-naming studies and messaging optimization are high-value research
Abrysvo (Maternal) Nirsevimab (Sanofi/AZ) Zero-sum with nirsevimab for infant RSV protection; monoclonal winning in US (38% vs. 19% coverage) Maternal market trajectory shapes how much PET/HCPP investment makes sense on this indication long-term
Prevnar 20 Capvaxive (Merck PCV21) "21 > 20" serotype counting narrative actively eroding Prevnar 20 in head-to-head conversations; Prevnar 20's serotype 4 advantage is never communicated Capvaxive competitive tracking is the most urgent research gap; PET-style study to quantify serotype counting prevalence
Prevnar 20 VAX-31 (Vaxcyte, Phase 3) 31-valent, >95% IPD coverage, FDA Breakthrough Therapy — potential disruptor post-2027 Long-term threat; monitoring only for now
Penbraya GSK pentavalent (MenABCWY) Both ACIP recommended; commercial battle is over formulary positioning and pediatrician habit Low MenB baseline coverage creates growth opportunity for whichever pentavalent wins habit formation

Key Relationships

Established / Repeat Business

  • Antonietta Forte — Director, BAI, Vaccines (Abrysvo OA PET)
  • Gabby Gonzalez — Meningococcal (HCPP, ATU, Digital Tracking in discussion)
  • Tim Douglas — Abrysvo Maternal (PET, HCPP, Digital Tracking) & Prevnar Adult (HCPP)

Above Brand

  • Aimee Renaud — AI Excellence (Message Labs AI Migraine Pilot)

Warm Contacts to Build

  • Gavin Hansen (Paxlovid) — warm via mutual referral
  • Stephanie Archer (Prevnar Peds) — already pitched HCPP
  • Josh Stewart (rare disease) — path to Vyndaquel/specialty
  • Candace Anderson (obesity) — long-term
  • Renata Alves (Global Vaccines) — long-term global play

Lost Champion

  • Lisa Dixon (Abrysvo Maternal) — was responsible for referrals and exploring new offerings; departed

SWOT Analysis

Strengths

  • HCPP entrenchment: 4 different vaccine products using HCP-Patient Conversations — becoming a standard part of the insights toolkit
  • AI pioneer reputation: Established through Abrysvo PET AI work and Message Labs pilot; ZoomRx is seen as the AI-forward research partner
  • Portfolio-level findings: Cross-study insights (communication style, brand-agnostic recommendations, disease severity gaps) demonstrate strategic value beyond brand-level reporting
  • Strong profitability: 61% profitability on $302K base; no preferred vendor discount constraining margins
  • Trusted on vaccines: Repeat business from Tim Douglas and Gabby Gonzalez demonstrates established trust

Weaknesses

  • Lost champion: Lisa Dixon's departure removed the most active referral engine; no equivalent internal advocate currently
  • Vaccines concentration: 100% of FY25 revenue from a single TA; vulnerable to vaccine-specific budget events
  • No annual contracts: All SOWs are project-based; revenue requires active re-selling each cycle
  • Narrow contact map: Only 3 established contacts, all in vaccines; non-vaccine org chart largely unmapped
  • No Pfizer MR budget intelligence: Unlike J&J, no visibility into Pfizer's total MR spend or budget allocation philosophy

Opportunities

  • $125K "other brands" expansion: Paxlovid HCPP ($75K via Gavin Hansen), Nurtec HCPP ($75K via Aimee Renaud) — 2 wins fills the gap
  • Capvaxive competitive tracking: ZoomRx's HCPP data already documents the serotype counting threat; propose a PET-style competitive study
  • Pharmacist channel deep dive: Both pneumococcal and RSV data show pharmacy referral as a drop-off risk; pharmacist-focused HCPP is a greenfield opportunity
  • BI PET displacement: Zero-cost pilot on Trumenba or Prevnar Adult in Q2; win 1-2 PETs = $75-150K incremental
  • Sermo ATU displacement: Abrysvo Maternal ATU consolidation (PET + HCPP + ATU = one vendor, three lenses)
  • Oncology entry via Lorbrena: First foothold; Seagen acquisition expanded Pfizer oncology portfolio significantly
  • 50-64 age group research: ACIP expansion added ~30M adults; this segment needs different messaging than 65+

Threats

  • Re-orgs or budget cuts: Abrysvo Adult PET was lost to a budget cut in a prior year. Risk currently assessed as low.
  • CDC recommendation changes: Further narrowing of RSV or pneumococcal recommendations could shrink the addressable market and reduce research budgets
  • Nirsevimab dominance: If maternal Abrysvo market continues to erode, that brand's research spend may decline
  • VAX-31 disruption: If Vaxcyte's 31-valent PCV reaches market, both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive face existential competitive pressure
  • BI entrenchment on PETs: BI has deep, long-running PET relationships at Pfizer; displacement is harder than new-category wins

Active Brands & Revenue Summary

Brand AD FY25 Base FY26 Target Work Type
Abrysvo (Maternal) Nick $216,000 $225,000 PET: $150K, HCPP: $75K
Abrysvo (Older Adult) Nick $0 $75,000 PET: $75K
Prevnar (Adult) Nick $0 $75,000 HCPP: $75K
Prevnar (Pediatric) Nick $0 $0 --
Trumenba/Penbraya (Mening) Nick $86,250 $75,000 HCPP: $75K
Other brands Nick $0 $125,000 HCPP: $125K
Total $302,250 $575,000

Pfizer's Financial Context

Metric FY 2025 Notes
Total Revenue ~$63.6B Post-COVID normalization; non-COVID portfolio driving growth
Abrysvo Revenue ~$1B 136% operational growth Q4 2025; international expansion driving growth
Prevnar Family ~$6B+ 8% operational growth Q4 2025; 3 billionth dose milestone
Paxlovid Declining Post-pandemic normalization
Oncology (ex-Seagen) Ibrance ~$4B+; Padcev growing Seagen acquisition transforming oncology portfolio

What a New Principal Needs to Know (Fast Orientation)

  1. This is a vaccines account trying to become more. Everything you see today is vaccines. The strategic imperative is to replicate the vaccines playbook (HCPP entry, AI differentiation, upsell to PET/ATU) across primary care, specialty, and oncology. The Lorbrena PET and Message Labs pilot are the first two non-vaccine projects — their success or failure defines the account's trajectory.

  2. HCPP is the franchise. It's the wedge, the differentiator, and the proof point. Delivery quality on HCPP must be consistently exceptional because every other service sale starts with "look what HCPP showed us."

  3. Marketing leads are your buyers. This is NOT like J&J where Insights teams commission work. At Pfizer, marketing leads hold significant sway. Frame everything in marketing terms — messaging optimization, brand positioning, competitive displacement — not data collection terms.

  4. You cannot sell franchise deals. Each brand operates independently. Each sale is separate. The referral network (Tim Douglas recommending ZoomRx to other brand leads) is the growth engine, not top-down selling.

  5. The Capvaxive threat is your best BD lever. ZoomRx has primary data showing the serotype counting narrative eroding Prevnar 20 in real conversations. This is urgent intelligence that Pfizer needs. Use it to propose competitive tracking.

  6. Aimee Renaud is the key above-brand connector. She's in AI Excellence and can introduce ZoomRx to any brand team. The Message Labs pilot is your audition — deliver exceptionally and ask for intros.

Back to Accounts Hub | Home

Abrysvo (Maternal)
Nirsevimab (Sanofi/AZ)
Zero-sum with nirsevimab for infant RSV protection; monoclonal winning in US (38% vs. 19% coverage)
Maternal market trajectory shapes how much PET/HCPP investment makes sense on this indication long-term


Prevnar 20
Capvaxive (Merck PCV21)
"21 > 20" serotype counting narrative actively eroding Prevnar 20 in head-to-head conversations; Prevnar 20's serotype 4 advantage is never communicated
Capvaxive competitive tracking is the most urgent research gap; PET-style study to quantify serotype counting prevalence


Prevnar 20
VAX-31 (Vaxcyte, Phase 3)
31-valent, >95% IPD coverage, FDA Breakthrough Therapy — potential disruptor post-2027
Long-term threat; monitoring only for now


Penbraya
GSK pentavalent (MenABCWY)
Both ACIP recommended; commercial battle is over formulary positioning and pediatrician habit
Low MenB baseline coverage creates growth opportunity for whichever pentavalent wins habit formation

SWOT Analysis
Strengths

HCPP entrenchment: 4 different vaccine products using HCP-Patient Conversations — becoming a standard part of the insights toolkit
AI pioneer reputation: Established through Abrysvo PET AI work and Message Labs pilot; ZoomRx is seen as the AI-forward research partner
Portfolio-level findings: Cross-study insights (communication style, brand-agnostic recommendations, disease severity gaps) demonstrate strategic value beyond brand-level reporting
Strong profitability: 61% profitability on $302K base; no preferred vendor discount constraining margins
Trusted on vaccines: Repeat business from Tim Douglas and Gabby Gonzalez demonstrates established trust

Weaknesses

Lost champion: Lisa Dixon's departure removed the most active referral engine; no equivalent internal advocate currently
Vaccines concentration: 100% of FY25 revenue from a single TA; vulnerable to vaccine-specific budget events
No annual contracts: All SOWs are project-based; revenue requires active re-selling each cycle
Narrow contact map: Only 3 established contacts, all in vaccines; non-vaccine org chart largely unmapped
No Pfizer MR budget intelligence: Unlike J&J, no visibility into Pfizer's total MR spend or budget allocation philosophy

Opportunities

$125K "other brands" expansion: Paxlovid HCPP ($75K via Gavin Hansen), Nurtec HCPP ($75K via Aimee Renaud) — 2 wins fills the gap
Capvaxive competitive tracking: ZoomRx's HCPP data already documents the serotype counting threat; propose a PET-style competitive study
Pharmacist channel deep dive: Both pneumococcal and RSV data show pharmacy referral as a drop-off risk; pharmacist-focused HCPP is a greenfield opportunity
BI PET displacement: Zero-cost pilot on Trumenba or Prevnar Adult in Q2; win 1-2 PETs = $75-150K incremental
Sermo ATU displacement: Abrysvo Maternal ATU consolidation (PET + HCPP + ATU = one vendor, three lenses)
Oncology entry via Lorbrena: First foothold; Seagen acquisition expanded Pfizer oncology portfolio significantly
50-64 age group research: ACIP expansion added ~30M adults; this segment needs different messaging than 65+

Threats

Re-orgs or budget cuts: Abrysvo Adult PET was lost to a budget cut in a prior year. Risk currently assessed as low.
CDC recommendation changes: Further narrowing of RSV or pneumococcal recommendations could shrink the addressable market and reduce research budgets
Nirsevimab dominance: If maternal Abrysvo market continues to erode, that brand's research spend may decline
VAX-31 disruption: If Vaxcyte's 31-valent PCV reaches market, both Prevnar 20 and Capvaxive face existential competitive pressure
BI entrenchment on PETs: BI has deep, long-running PET relationships at Pfizer; displacement is harder than new-category wins
